steps.title: Calculate the confidence interval
steps.content:
Margin of error is ±3% around the point estimate of 30%.

Lower bound: 30% − 3% = 27%
Upper bound: 30% + 3% = 33%

The confidence interval is [27%, 33%]. This range represents where we expect the true population percent to fall.

steps.title: Evaluate each statement against the confidence interval
steps.content:
The confidence interval tells us which population values are plausible (inside the interval) and which are doubtful (outside the interval).

Choice A: Misinterprets 3% as a sample misclassification rate. Margin of error measures the uncertainty in our estimate when generalizing to the population, not classification accuracy within the sample.

Choice B: States it is not possible for the true percent to be below 27%. This language is too absolute; confidence intervals allow a small probability that the true parameter lies outside the bounds (typically 5% for a 95% CI).

Choice C: Claims the true percent is 33%. This confuses the upper boundary with the estimate itself. The true value is likely somewhere in [27%, 33%], not specifically 33%.

Choice D: States it is doubtful that the true percent is 35%. Since 35% > 33%, the value 35% lies outside the confidence interval [27%, 33%]. The word doubtful correctly reflects that values outside the interval are less likely. That matches choice D — D is correct.
